0: <header>Life cycle assessment of milk production - a comparison of conventional and organic farming </header>

1: An LCA was performed on organic and conventional milk production at the farm level in Sweden. In the study, special focus was aimed at substance flows in concentrate feed production and nutrient flows on the farms. The different feeding strategies in the two forms of production, influence several impact categories. The import of feed by conventional dairy farms often leads to a substantial input of phosphorus and nitrogen. Organic milk production is a way to reduce pesticide use and mineral surplus in agriculture but this production form also requires substantially more farmland than conventional production. For Swedish conditions, however, a large use of grassland for grazing ruminants is regarded positively since this type of arable land use promotes the domestic environmental goals of biodiversity and aesthetic values. © 2000 Elsevier Science Ltd. All rights reserved.
Keywords: LCA; Milk production; Organic farming
2: The market for organically-produced food has shown a drastic increase during the 1990s in Sweden. Milk has the largest sale of organic food articles and in 1998, 3% of the milk sales were labelled organic. The prognoses from the largest dairy industry predict that this will be doubled in the year 2000. There is not only a consumer interest for these products but also a political one. In 1994, the Swedish parliament decided that one environ- mental target for Swedish agriculture was to obtain an organic production corresponding to 10% of all arable land by the year 2000. In a future scenario, the Swedish EPA has designated that as much as 75% of Swedish milk should be produced according to organic principles by the year 2021 [1]. One important way of encouraging this development politically is through the European Union's Common Agricultural Policy, which gives fin- ancial support to a more environmentally sound agricul- ture. In Sweden, organic farming is a key element in this
program, and receives yearly financial support ranging from 900-1600 SEK per hectare.

This article is based on two studies "Life Cycle Assessment of Milk Production - A Comparison of Conventional and Organic Farming" and "Life Cycle Assessment of Conventional and Organic Consumer Milk" which were carried out at the SIK, the Swedish Institute for Food and Biotechnology and financed by the Swedish Farmers Foundation for Agricultural Research, the Swedish Farmers Union and the Swedish Dairy Association [2,3].

2.3. Descriptions of systems in the study

Conventional milk production in Sweden is character- ised by intense production and the dairy COWS are among the highest yielding in Europe. In 1996 the number of cows was 466,625, and during the 1990s the intensity in feeding has increased along with a rising milk yield. One important environmental law regulating animal pro- duction in Sweden concerns livestock density per hec- tare. The upper limit for dairy farms is 1.6 dairy cows per hectare.

Organic milk is produced according to the eco-label- ling system KRAV, which is the sole environmental labelling system for organic milk in Sweden. In 1997, 352 farms with a total of 11,259 COWS produced organic milk, and another 100 farms will be changing to this form of production in 1998/99. Characteristics for this form of milk production are:

<list>Concerning fodder, there are limits for the use of con- ventionally-produced feed (maximum 5%). On a yearly basis, 50% of fodder (DM)2 must be produced on the farm itself. During the grazing period, pasture must be at least half of the total feed intake; 
In the production of crops, no synthetic fertilisers or pesticides are allowed; 
If medicines are used, the waiting-time (i.e. the time- period between the medicine intake and the milking of the treated cow for milk delivery) must be double that of conventional production; 
The natural behaviour of the animals shall be pro- moted and during the grazing period most of the days shall be spent outdoors. Cows shall be able to give birth in a private loose box and calves shall be given non-processed natural milk for the first 10 weeks.</list>
The demand for a large amounts of farm-produced fod- der in organic milk production leads to lower livestock density in this production system.

7: <header>2.4. Studied objects </header>

Data were collected from two relatively large dairy farms in the west of Sweden which are run by an Agric- ulture Society and follow a current commercial pro- duction scheme. These farms were chosen since there were production data of very high quality (e.g. all fodder is weighed), and because they are specialised milk pro- ducers, which means there are no other co-products except meat from the farms. Another important reason for choosing two individual enterprises was that there are no Swedish statistics available on average energy use and land use in different milk production systems. Both farms grow all roughage fodder for their own use which is customary for Swedish dairy farms. The use of con- centrate feed on the conventional farm is high but such is the case for many Swedish conventional dairy farms today. The use of diesel in the farms' crop production and the use of fertiliser on the conventional farm have been compared with data from other farms and appear to be within a normal range. The yield of grain and peas on the organic farm is relatively low which probably leads to a proportionately large use of arable land.

As roughage fodder, the conventional farm used 2267 kg DM grass silage and 687 kg DM pressed beet pulp³ per cow. The organic farm used both silage and hay as roughage fodder, 1869 kg DM and 1355 kg respectively per cow. The intake of pasture was 775 kg DM per organic cow, more than double that of the conventional farm. The grain consumption was 25% higher per cow on the conventional farm. While the organic farm used 1000 kg peas per cow (produced on the farm and on a neighbouring farm) and purchased concentrate feed4 corresponding to 343 kg per cow, the conventional farm used 1531 kg purchased concentrate feed per cow. On the conventional farm each cow produced 7.813 FU and on the organic farm the milk production was 7.127 FU per cow.

Buildings and machinery were excluded in the analy- sis. Stables and farm buildings have a similar design on the farms studied. Also excluded in the study were medicines, washing detergents and minor stable supplies such as disinfec- tants, salt for cows, etc.

12: There is a strong causality between the size and qual- ity of the feed ration of the dairy cow and her two output products milk and meat. The allocation between these two products was based on this causality. Calculation according to Swedish fodder tables [4] for the supply of energy and protein to cover the dairy cow's milk- production, her maintenance and pregnancy gave an overall allocation of 85% to milk and 15% to meat [2]. Since all manure stayed on the farms and was used as fertiliser in the crop production this was not an output product and therefore no allocation was needed for the manure.

Conventional concentrate feed is made from co-pro- ducts from the production of vegetable oils, starch and sugar. This allocation was based on the economic value of the products (world market price). When the co-pro- ducts have a very small value, less than 5%, only the direct processing and transport of the feed co-product (e.g. the transport of palm kernel expel) were allocated to the feed.
When calculating the area of farmland needed to pro- duce milk, mass allocation was used. With this calcu-

lation, all the sold products from one crop were con- sidered, for example, one average hectare of Swedish sugar beets yields 47 ton sugar beet which gives three products: 7100 kg sugar, 2970 kg DM super pressed pulp and 600 kg DM molasses, for a total of 10670 kg pro- ducts. Super pressed pulp (see note 2) is used as a rough- age fodder and can basically substitute grass/clover sil- age on a mass basis (kg DM by kg DM). By the allocation procedure it becomes obvious how area- efficient the sugar beet crop is. In addition to obtaining 7100 kg sugar from one hectare of sugar beets, a rough- age fodder is also yielded which, in practice, can substi- tute almost 0.5 hectare of leys for silage production.
A summary of price and mass allocation for concen- trate feed components is shown in Table 1, where the origin of the feed ingredients is also shown.

14: The environmental impact categories considered in the study were:

<list>Resources: energy, material and land use; 
Human health: pesticide use; 
Ecological effects: global warming, acidification, eutrophication, photo-oxidant formation and depletion of stratospheric ozone.</list>

The widespread use of pesticides is one of the major impacts of agricultural production. In addition to poss- ible human exposure, pesticide use can also cause eco- toxicological impacts in aquatic and terrestrial systems. Indirectly, discharges of pesticides may also contribute to decreased biodiversity. Most of the methods for tox- icity assessment need more elaboration which is further

discussed by Audsley et al. [5]. In this analysis, the quantitative use of pesticides per FU is calculated and a red-flag classification is performed. The base for the red- flagging is pesticides which are prohibited in Swedish agriculture.

Andersson shows that emission of cadmium from fer- tilisers is an important hot-spot in food production sys- tems [6]. This impact was not assessed in this work because the conventional farm studied (like the great majority of dairy farms in Sweden) does not use any commercial P-fertilisers since the manure supplies an adequate amount of P. The use of P-fertiliser in the con- ventional milk life-cycle is instead found on the arable farms that grow the crops for concentrate feed pro- duction. The import of feed, which is often substantial on a conventional milk farm, leads to a phosphorus inflow most of which will stay on the farm and be spread on the arable land with the farmyard manure. The input of cadmium to the dairy farm can be traced to the feed import and the atmospheric deposition. Similar to phos- phorus, the lion's share of the cadmium will remain on the farm in the manure since the output products milk and meat contain and remove minute amounts of heavy metals. The Swedish data available on cadmium content in fodder and manure are very scarce. A cadmium bal- ance presented by Cederberg showed a large variation in the limited data material SO far available [7]. Although no assessment of cadmium was conducted here, it seems evident that farming systems with a large input of feed, and which only have animal output products, run a potential risk of accumulating heavy metals in the soil. Examples of omitted impact categories are water use, soil quality, eco-toxicological impacts, habitat alterations and impacts on biological diversity.

1: The following criteria, which are taken from the Nordic Guidelines for Life Cycle Assessment, were used as a check-list for data quality assessment [8].
2: Data from the farms studied are measured data and are related to milk production only with the exception of electricity consumption which was divided between milk production and other activities on the farms by means of estimations. Data from the feed and oil extrac- tion industry in Sweden and Norway were calculated data based on measurements. Data from the oil extrac- tion industry outside the Nordic countries were based on energy use in industries with best available technique (BAT). Data on emissions and use of resources in ferti- liser production come from a thorough inventory of the most commonly used fertilisers in Sweden and Western Europe [9]. Data on cultivation of crops outside Sweden for the concentrate feed production are based on quali- fied estimates by agricultural experts and statistics.
3: The farms in this study and the Swedish feed and oil extraction industry are enterprises with an interest in the study and they have provided verified information, e.g. through company environmental reports. Nutrient emis- sions in Sweden were calculated with verified infor- mation from public authorities. Cultivation data for crops outside Sweden were mostly given by growers' associ- ations.
4: The data collection method of this study was to choose a small number of enterprises (two farms) with high quality data based on continuous measurements. The fodder consumption per 1000 kg ECM has been compared to similar producers and is within a reasonable range. The feeding system used by both farms (mixer wagon and a whole mixed fodder) can give 5-10% higher consumption than a system where each cow is fed individually. Also the loose housing system where the cow can move around and exercise outdoors in an exercise yard all year around, can contribute to a higher fodder consumption than in a tie-up cow-house.
5: All data used are of recent date, going back no further than 1995. Due to an increasing interest in the pro- duction background of the imported commodities to the Swedish feed industry, the aim was to obtain specific data on each important oil/starch crop where the co-pro- ducts are imported for production of concentrate feeds.
6: The farms studied were chosen because of their equiv- alence in technology and processes.
7: <header>3. Results </header>
3.1. Resources: energy, material and land

The use of primary energy was 3550 MJ per FU in the conventional system and 2511 MJ per FU in the organic system. The results can, to a great extent, be explained by the difference of feeding strategy and the use of syn- thetic fertilisers in conventional production. The substan- tially greater input of concentrate feed in conventional milk production leads to a higher energy demand. Dan- ish investigations also show lower energy requirements on organic dairy farms, 2160 MJ/1000 kg milk in organic production and 3340 MJ/1000 kg milk in con- ventional production [10].

In Table 2, the systems' use of materials is shown. Since machinery is not included in the analysis, the material use is very much dependent on energy and ferti- liser consumption. The organic farm has a higher con- sumption of electricity per cow and FU, which is the explanation for the larger use of uranium and hydro- power. The total use of crude oil was slightly higher in the conventional system but the use of diesel was sub- stantially larger in the organic system. This is mainly because of a larger use of tractor diesel at the farm level due to a larger fodder production and lower yields on the organic farm. The considerably larger use of natural gas in the conventional system is explained by the syn- thetic N-fertilisers. The difference in coal consumption is due to the refining of components in the concentrate feed, mainly the drying of beet fibres which are a pri- mary ingredient in concentrate feed. The use of phos- phorus on the organic farm is mainly due to the purchase of mineral feed.

11: In Table 3, a summary of pesticides used in the culti- vation of fodder crops is shown. The red-flagging of soy- meal is due to a number of toxic pesticides in soybean cultivation. Monocrotofos and endosulfan are two com-

monly used insecticides, both substances being highly toxic [11]. The herbicide 2,4-D was prohibited in Sweden in 1991 because of suspected carcinogenic effects on workers using this pesticide. Atrazine, which is the standard herbicide used in maize on the European continent as well as in the US, was prohibited in Sweden in 1992 due to its high soil mobility and persistence lead- ing to a high risk for groundwater contamination.

The use of pesticides (as active substances) per FU was 118 g in the conventional system and 10.8 g in the organic system. Herbicides make up approximately 90% of total pesticide use, while insecticides and fungicides make up 5% each of total use. One aspect worth con- sidering is that the intense use of chemicals in soybean cropping has a direct impact on insecticide use in con- ventional milk's life cycle. Due to the large use of soy- meal in the conventional concentrate feed, as much as 75% of the insecticide use per FU in this production form is explained by insecticide application in soybeans.

13: Some of the most important ecological effects of mod- ern agriculture, especially animal production, are related to non-functioning nutrient cycles which entail losses of nitrogen pollutants. Nutrient balance is a useful tool when quantifying the flow of nutrients in food pro- duction systems. A model for this is shown in Fig. 3; nutrient balances were calculated for the farms studied. The computer program STANK from the National Board of Agriculture was used for contents of plant nutrients in products (feed, fertilisers, milk, etc.) [12]. The bio- logical N-fixation was estimated on the basis of the crops and the amount of legumes [13]. N-deposition on arable land in western Sweden was based on measurements provided by the environmental section of the County Board.

N emissions on the farms studied are shown. Nitrate leaching and ammonia volatilisation were calculated using recent Swedish models for N-losses, NO3-N losses according to Johnsson and and Hoffman [16] and NH- N according to the computer program STANK [12]. N20-N losses were calculated using emission factors from IPCC [17]. In Table 5, the quantified losses are summed up. As seen, a smaller part of the N-surplus was found as emissions on the conventional farm.

Nitrogen lost through denitrification is another important loss of N from the agriculture system. Since N2 is a natural component of the atmosphere no environ- mental damage results from this loss. However, denitri- fication may be one explanation as to why a smaller part of the N surplus was found as emissions on the conven- tional farm (48%). This farm has clay soils and much larger N-flows than the organic farm and it is reasonable that the denitrification has been as high as 30-50 kg N ha- The organic farm in the study has lighter soils and low flows of mineral-N in the soil and denitrification losses should not exceed 10 kg N ha [18]. However, even when denitrification of this magnitude is added to total N-emissions there is still a share of N-surplus that is not accounted for: 15-20% on the organic farm and 25-40% on the conventional farm. Input of N in the soil pool could be one explanation of the discrepancy. Long- term field experiments in Sweden show that continuous application of farmyard manure has caused a greater increase of total nitrogen in soils than other types of fer- tilisers; as long as 35 years after application, a steady state has not yet been reached [19]. However, it may well be that the N-losses are underestimated since the different guidelines for calculating nutrient losses are quite rough and in practice large individual differences may occur. When evaluating the different models used here for calculating losses of nitrate, ammonia and nitrous oxide, it seems likely that it is the calculations of ammonia emissions that are the most uncertain ones and probably underestimated in the conventional system. New Dutch research shows a very strong connection between a high feeding intensity of protein and high NH3-losses [20]. The use of high protein feed is much greater in the conventional system. A Swedish national report also points out the increasing intensity in dairy cow feeding as one cause of increasing ammonia emis- sions in Sweden [21].

Comparing the calculated nutrient losses in an LCA study with a nutrient balance is an appropriate way to validate the data and emission factors used in the calcu- lations. For nitrogen, which can be found is SO many different forms in soil, air and water, it is common that all N surplus cannot be explained as N emissions. For example, of a total N-surplus of 190,000 tons in Swedish agriculture, 154,000 tons are defined as losses, denitrifi- cation and soil input and this leaves 20% unaccounted for [22]. In the Danish N balance for 1996/97, 78,000 tons N out of a total surplus of 409,000 tons N was unaccounted for [23]. Also in Dutch nitrogen balances, all N output emissions cannot be accounted for, and in balance calculations this is often added to the soil pool [24,25].

As can be seen in Table 5, milk production per hectare is more than twice as much on the conventional farm compared with the organic farm. This is due to a higher animal density, which is the result of the use of imported feed and fertilisers. From this it follows that when the N-losses per hectare are converted into N-losses per FU (1000 kg milk) the difference between the two systems of milk production is smaller, than is the case when only studying the area-based nutrient balance.

Table 6 shows the calculated N-losses per FU on the farms in the study but it is important to note that this does not cover all the emissions of reactive nitrogen in the milk's life cycle. About 40% of the total nitrate emis- sions in the milk's life cycle occur outside the farm boundary on the arable land where the purchased feed was grown. Basically all ammonia emissions in the milk's life cycle take place at the farm level in close connection with the farmyard manure. In the conven- tional milk's life cycle almost 50% of the nitrous oxide emissions take place outside the farm boundary, mostly as process emissions in fertiliser production.

As can be seen in Table 5, milk production per hectare is more than twice as much on the conventional farm compared with the organic farm. This is due to a higher animal density, which is the result of the use of imported feed and fertilisers. From this it follows that when the N-losses per hectare are converted into N-losses per FU (1000 kg milk) the difference between the two systems of milk production is smaller, than is the case when only studying the area-based nutrient balance.

Table 6 shows the calculated N-losses per FU on the farms in the study but it is important to note that this does not cover all the emissions of reactive nitrogen in the milk's life cycle. About 40% of the total nitrate emis- sions in the milk's life cycle occur outside the farm boundary on the arable land where the purchased feed was grown. Basically all ammonia emissions in the milk's life cycle take place at the farm level in close connection with the farmyard manure. In the conven- tional milk's life cycle almost 50% of the nitrous oxide emissions take place outside the farm boundary, mostly as process emissions in fertiliser production.

N emissions on the farms studied are shown. Nitrate leaching and ammonia volatilisation were calculated using recent Swedish models for N-losses, NO3-N losses according to Johnsson and and Hoffman [16] and NH- N according to the computer program STANK [12]. N20-N losses were calculated using emission factors from IPCC [17]. In Table 5, the quantified losses are summed up. As seen, a smaller part of the N-surplus was found as emissions on the conventional farm.

Nitrogen lost through denitrification is another important loss of N from the agriculture system. Since N2 is a natural component of the atmosphere no environ- mental damage results from this loss. However, denitri- fication may be one explanation as to why a smaller part of the N surplus was found as emissions on the conven- tional farm (48%). This farm has clay soils and much larger N-flows than the organic farm and it is reasonable that the denitrification has been as high as 30-50 kg N ha- The organic farm in the study has lighter soils and low flows of mineral-N in the soil and denitrification losses should not exceed 10 kg N ha [18]. However, even when denitrification of this magnitude is added to total N-emissions there is still a share of N-surplus that is not accounted for: 15-20% on the organic farm and 25-40% on the conventional farm. Input of N in the soil pool could be one explanation of the discrepancy. Long- term field experiments in Sweden show that continuous application of farmyard manure has caused a greater increase of total nitrogen in soils than other types of fer- tilisers; as long as 35 years after application, a steady state has not yet been reached [19]. However, it may well be that the N-losses are underestimated since the different guidelines for calculating nutrient losses are quite rough and in practice large individual differences may occur. When evaluating the different models used here for calculating losses of nitrate, ammonia and nitrous oxide, it seems likely that it is the calculations of ammonia emissions that are the most uncertain ones and probably underestimated in the conventional system. New Dutch research shows a very strong connection between a high feeding intensity of protein and high NH3-losses [20]. The use of high protein feed is much greater in the conventional system. A Swedish national report also points out the increasing intensity in dairy cow feeding as one cause of increasing ammonia emis- sions in Sweden [21].

Comparing the calculated nutrient losses in an LCA study with a nutrient balance is an appropriate way to validate the data and emission factors used in the calcu- lations. The most important contributor to global warming in milk production is, however, meth- ane. The discharges were calculated and weighted to 17.98 kg SO2-equivalents per FU in the conventional system and 15.81 SO2-equivalents per FU in the organic system and it is clear that ammonia is the key parameter. The acidification potential is approximately 10% lower in the organic system. 1: The calculation of the important nutrifying substances nitrate and ammonia was described earlier. Estimations of P-losses are based on data with high uncertainty since there are great variations in P-losses from arable land. The average loss in Sweden is 0.32 kg P ha- but the variation is as high as 0.01-2.61 kg P ha- [30]. Further- more, there are no data on differences in P-losses between organic and conventional farming. In this study, P-losses were estimated to be 0.35 P kg ha on the conventional farm and 0.25 kg P ha- on the organic farm. This assumption was based on the fact that both the manure application rate and the P-surplus are higher on the conventional farm, which lead to a higher P- accumulation in the soils.

Although the organic farm has a lower nitrate leaching per hectare (see Table 5), the nitrate loss per FU is higher. There are two explanations for this. Firstly, the overall yields are generally lower in the organic system but the nitrate losses per hectare in this system do not seem to be sufficiently low to compensate the lower yields. Consequently, the nitrate loss per kg fodder for grain and silage was higher in this system. Secondly, the choice of concentrate feed differs. The organic farm uses a considerable amount of peas, and nitrate leaching in relation to yield is rather high for this feed. The conven- tional farm, on the other hand, uses significant amounts of purchased concentrate feed which have lower nitrate discharges per kg feed. Since these purchased concen- trate feeds are based on co-products from oil/starch/sugar crops, the nitrate leaching from these crops is divided through the allocation procedures between the main pro- duct and the co-products.

The potential eutrophication was found by using the maximum scenario method described by Heijungs et al. [29] and Lindfors et al. [8]. The result is shown in Fig. 5. As previously described, the ammonia emissions might be underestimated in the conventional system and this uncertainty makes it hard to reach a clear-cut con- clusion concerning the differences between the two alter-
Fig. 5. The maximum potential contribution to eutrophication, kg O2- equivalents per FU.

natives. Discharges of phosphate seem to have a very small impact on the potential eutrophication from milk at present. 4: Due to the higher use of tractor diesel per FU on the organic farm studied, the emissions of NOx, catalyst of the photo-oxidant reaction, is greater. This greater diesel consumption also results in larger emissions of CO and hydrocarbons. Actually when classifying all emissions that can contribute to this impact category, only hexane discharges (from the oil extraction industry) are higher for the conventional system. All other parameters important for photo-oxidant formation show higher emissions in the organic system.
5: Although machinery was not included in the analysis, the cooling medias in dairy farms' milk cooler equip- ment are worth mentioning. On Swedish dairy farms, all cooler tanks containing R12 have been replaced and dur- ing 1998 cooler tanks containing R22 are to be replaced. Compounds such as methane, nitrous oxide, carbon monoxide can directly or indirectly influence strato- spheric ozone depletion. Due to the complexity and the incomplete understanding of the processes, no ODPs were calculated for these compounds and no characteris- ation was therefore done. But it is evident that losses of nitrogen compounds are also vital for this impact cate- gory; it has been estimated that doubling the concen- tration of nitrous oxide in the atmosphere would result in a 10% decrease in the ozone layer and this would increase the ultraviolet radiation reaching the earth by 20% [31].
6: Hot-spots were chiefly identified from current dis- cussions and studies on important environmental goals for the Swedish agriculture of the future [1].

Concerning the use of materials, phosphorous should be highlighted as being a limited resource and almost exclusively used in agriculture as fertiliser and fodder mineral. Long-term sustainable food production must deal responsibly with this element. As seen in Table 2, the use of phosphorous per FU is higher in the conven- tional system due to fertiliser application in the feed imported to the farm. Moreover, it should be noted that

the method of dividing co-products from the cultivation of oil/starch/sugar crops according to price allocation puts the P-flow in the conventional system in a better light than it is in actual practice. The P-flow is more likely to follow the mass when it is divided between the co-products from the crops that provide the raw material for the feed industry. In this respect, the nutrient balance gives more correct information on the true phosphorous flow. As seen in Table 4, the conventional farm shows a substantially higher P-surplus per hectare. There are two important environmental aspects to consider in terms of this finding. Firstly, it signifies an inefficient use of this limited resource. Feed is exported from arable farms to animal producing farms and P-fertiliser must replace nutrient losses following the harvest products from arable farms. Secondly, accumulation of plant- available P in soil can lead to a future leaching problem. Recent research from The Broadbalk Experiment at Rothamstead, UK, where different fertiliser P levels have been applied in wheat field trials for more than 100 years, shows increasing P-leaching from plots where an excess of P in fertilisers or manure has been applied for an extended period of time [32]. On the basis of these findings it is probably not sufficient to assess merely the momentary environmental effects of phosphorous losses from agricultural systems, but also the long term effects of P accumulation in soils should be taken into account. Emissions of the nitrogen pollutants ammonia and nitrate are major sources for eutrophication and acidifi- cation, being hot-spots in both of the systems studied and generally connected to most agricultural production, especially the production of milk, meat and eggs. Ammonia, being an acidifying as well as nutrifying com- pound, is closely connected to the handling of farmyard manure and a number of measures have been and will be implemented to decrease ammonia emission from the agricultural sector in Sweden. The need for these measurements is underscored by the results of this study, showing that the acidifying emissions due to the use of fossil fuels are almost negligible in comparison with the key parameter ammonia. The greater nitrate leaching per FU in the organic system shows that a low input system is not automatically a guarantee for a lower environmen- tal impact. There is always a "base leaching" from agri- cultural land and the substantially higher area needed to produce organic milk leads to a relatively higher nitrate loss per kg organically produced grain and peas. The use of arable land to produce milk was substan- tially larger on the organic farm studied. Although there is an excess of arable land in Europe today, leading to a set-aside program in the EU, the larger area occupancy must be seen as a hot-spot for organic food production systems. However, it is not sufficient to evaluate the land resource in merely quantitative terms. A number of environmental goals are connected to arable land use and apart from direct impacts such as soil erosion and soil

compacting, indirect effects of land use must be con- sidered. Indirect effects are of a more subjective nature and contain aspects of aesthetic values such as the pres- ervation of the beauty of scenery, open landscapes and cultural heritage. For example, one important problem connected to agricultural land use in Sweden today is the lack of grazing ruminants preserving valuable biotopes [33].

One opinion often expressed is that intense production systems can liberate areas for growing energy crops. Had the organic farm in this analysis followed a conventional production scheme using N-fertilisers and more imported feed components, Salix (willow) could have been cultivated on the surplus area. This option, how- ever, strongly conflicts with environmental goals for the agriculture in this region, the conditions of which are typical for a large part of Swedish milk production. The farm is situated in a rural area where open landscapes and biotopes connected to grazing ruminants are highly valued and relinquishment of arable land is considered a problem. Financial support according to the EU environmental programme is given for growing leys and grassland in order to preserve arable land in this region [34]. In this food production system, a larger land use, especially of grassland, actually promotes important Swedish environmental goals.

Pesticide use is a hot-spot, and in conventional agri- cultural production in Sweden today great efforts are being made to reduce pesticide use and to ensure the handling of pesticides through strict legislation. This analysis, going to the cradle of the imported protein feeds, shows that environmental impact from pesticide use abroad should also be considered if the long term goal of environment-friendly production can be fulfilled.

Emissions of the potent greenhouse gases methane and nitrous oxide are critical parameters when assessing agricultural production. The relations between methane emissions and feeding strategy appear to be an important issue requiring further investigation. From present knowledge it can be concluded that organic milk pro- duction should not be too extensive in terms of a large use of roughage fodder. Concerning the use of materials, phosphorous should be highlighted as being a limited resource and almost exclusively used in agriculture as fertiliser and fodder mineral. Long-term sustainable food production must deal responsibly with this element. As seen in Table 2, the use of phosphorous per FU is higher in the conven- tional system due to fertiliser application in the feed imported to the farm. Moreover, it should be noted that

the method of dividing co-products from the cultivation of oil/starch/sugar crops according to price allocation puts the P-flow in the conventional system in a better light than it is in actual practice. The P-flow is more likely to follow the mass when it is divided between the co-products from the crops that provide the raw material for the feed industry. In this respect, the nutrient balance gives more correct information on the true phosphorous flow. As seen in Table 4, the conventional farm shows a substantially higher P-surplus per hectare. There are two important environmental aspects to consider in terms of this finding. Firstly, it signifies an inefficient use of this limited resource. Secondly, accumulation of plant- available P in soil can lead to a future leaching problem. Recent research from The Broadbalk Experiment at Rothamstead, UK, where different fertiliser P levels have been applied in wheat field trials for more than 100 years, shows increasing P-leaching from plots where an excess of P in fertilisers or manure has been applied for an extended period of time [32]. compacting, indirect effects of land use must be con- sidered. 11: Suggested improvements in conventional production are: changes in concentrate feed; improving the farm nutrient flows to reduce nutrient surplus, and demands for less use of toxic pesticides in imported concentrate feeds. An increased use of domestic/regionally produced rape-seed meal instead of feed ingredients imported from

other continents would lower both energy use and pestic- ide use. If the self-supporting capacity of fodder could increase on conventional farms, there would be a better interaction between fodder production and manure appli- cation and the P-flow would be more efficient. Large input of high-protein concentrate feed should be avoided to reduce nitrogen surplus and ammonia emissions. Through its purchase of feed components, the feed industry could promote the development of integrated soybean cropping in which toxic pesticides could be replaced by biological substances [35].

Suggested improvements in organic production are: differentiation in limits of purchased feed and an increase in the self-supporting capacity of the farm. The life cycles of concentrate feeds and their environmental burden should be considered when establishing the limit for the use of conventionally produced feed in organic production. One way is to use a differentiation and to allow a higher limit of feed with less environmental bur- den, of which rape-seed meal and peas are good examples. A greater use of concentrate feed in organic milk production (providing this feed has a relatively low environmental burden) could also reduce emissions of methane since these are connected to feed composition and quality. Also organic dairy farms should endeavour to attain a high self-supporting capacity of fodder. One way is to reduce the replacement rate SO that fewer hei- fers have to be fed. Another way of achieving this is to cultivate crops which can produce a great deal of fodder on small areas. Fodder beets are such a crop. This meas- ure could contribute to lower nitrate loss per FU on the farm in this study, since the imported organic grain and peas to the farm studied were responsible for a consider- able part of the nitrate loss in the milk's life cycle.

13: This study shows that a low-input agricultural system, such as organic milk production, has obvious environ- mental benefits. The most apparent environmental bene- fits are the considerably reduced use of pesticides and phosphorus. Concerning other environmental impacts, e.g. global warming, acidification and eutrophication, it appears that measures to reduce the potential impact from milk production need to be implemented in both systems analysed. It is also evident that when comparing the environmental performance of conventional and organic food production systems which have such differ- ences in material and energy flows, land use must be assessed in both quantitative and qualitative terms. The aesthetic values and biodiversity connected to Swedish arable land use were briefly discussed in this paper but other important criteria of qualitative land use such as soil erosion, soil organic matter and soil compaction should also be addressed when evaluating the use of the resource arable land.
